page: Microsoft Azure F32-8ams_v7 is an x86_64 architecture server belonging to the Famsv7 family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 9V45 processor operating at 4.5 GHz. The server is equipped with 256.0 GB of memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 32.0 GB per core. It has no local storage and contains zero GPUs.

Benchmark data indicates top-tier single-core CPU performance, with stress-ng single-core CPU performance, gzip compression, and bzip3 decompression all ranking in the top 10%. However, multi-core performance is more moderate, with stress-ng multi-core CPU performance, PassMark CPU scores, and multi-threaded bzip3 decompression falling into the average category. Memory bandwidth tests for both small cached blocks and large uncached blocks also yield average results. For language model workloads, the server demonstrates top-tier text generation speeds for small 135M models, but performance drops to poor for prompt processing on large 70B models.

The qualitative cost profile of this server reflects its high memory density relative to its compute resources. With 32.0 GB of RAM per vCPU, it offers high memory capacity without requiring a high vCPU count, which can be cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads that do not scale with core counts. However, the lack of local storage and GPUs, combined with average multi-core and memory bandwidth performance, represents a tradeoff for storage-heavy or parallelized compute tasks. This server is suited for memory-intensive applications, database operations, and small-scale language model inference.
description: Microsoft Azure F32-8ams_v7 is a memory-intensive x86_64 server in the Famsv7 family, running on Microsoft Hyper-V. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 9V45 processor at 4.5 GHz, paired with 256.0 GB of memory, which provides a high ratio of 32.0 GB of RAM per core. The server has no local storage or GPU accelerators. In benchmarks, it exhibits top-tier single-core CPU performance and single-threaded decompression speeds, alongside average multi-core and memory bandwidth results. This hardware profile is cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads that do not require high core counts. It is suited for database operations, memory-intensive applications, and small-scale language model inference.
